- Retrospective.
- Knowledge captured.

---

# Decision Gates

Each phase must satisfy predefined exit criteria.

| Gate | Validation |
|------|------------|
| Gate 1 | Preparation completed |
| Gate 2 | Analysis approved |
| Gate 3 | Design validated |
| Gate 4 | Final approval |
| Gate 5 | Documentation completed |

The next phase must not begin until the current gate has been approved.

---

# Success Criteria

The playbook is considered successful when:

- Objectives have been achieved.
- Deliverables have been completed.
- Stakeholders approve the outcome.
- Documentation has been updated.

---

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|------------|
| Example | Example |

---

# Metrics

| Metric | Target |
|---------|---------|
| Completion Rate | 100% |
| Documentation Coverage | ≥95% |
| Stakeholder Satisfaction | ≥4/5 |

---

# Quality Checklist

Before closing the playbook verify:

- [ ] Objectives achieved.
- [ ] Deliverables completed.
- [ ] Architecture documented.
- [ ] Decisions recorded.
- [ ] Risks documented.
- [ ] Lessons learned captured.
- [ ] Knowledge transferred.

---

# Best Practices

- Keep activities outcome-oriented.
- Document decisions as they occur.
- Involve stakeholders early.
- Validate frequently.
- Prefer small iterative improvements.

---

# Anti-patterns

Avoid:

- Skipping preparation.
- Starting implementation without validation.
- Closing without documentation.
- Missing stakeholder participation.
- Treating the playbook as a checklist instead of a guide.

# Final Statement

Playbooks transform architectural principles into repeatable execution.

Their purpose is not to prescribe every action, but to provide a proven path that teams can adapt while maintaining consistency, quality and governance.

---

> **Standards define expectations.**
>
> **Playbooks enable execution.**
>
> **Execution creates results.**
